narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles from thousands of past students. The model looked at your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, LSAT score of 26, Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings both at the 1st decile, upper-middle family income, and your plan to attend full-time. It predicted a 78% probability that you would fail the bar exam initially, which is why your application was rejected—law schools use this to prioritize students with stronger predicted odds of success. The three biggest factors driving this prediction were your Year 3 decile ranking, your undergraduate GPA, and your LSAT score, in that order of influence. Most significantly, your Year 3 decile ranking in the bottom 1st percentile—far below the typical student's 5th or 6th decile—strongly pushed the model toward predicting bar failure, signaling to it that your later academic performance raised serious concerns about exam readiness. In contrast, your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, while slightly below the average of 3.21, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction toward passing, showing some solid foundational strength. Your LSAT score of 26, however, which is well below the dataset average of about 37, pulled the prediction back toward failure, as lower scores like yours often correlate with bar struggles in the model's view. Compared to typical students, who often have mid-range deciles, higher LSATs around 37, and similar GPAs, your profile stood out negatively due to those low deciles and LSAT, despite your full-time commitment aligning with the vast majority. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male from an upper-middle background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements—but the model weighs these metrics heavily for bar passage. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost it toward 35 or higher, strengthening your recent grades to climb those deciles, and perhaps seeking academic coaching or bar prep resources tailored for high-achieving applicants like you. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds dramatically, opening doors at competitive programs. You're clearly driven, and with these steps, you can rewrite this story.
